title: remove need to download pause image
body:
So far, the infra containers of pods required pulling down an image
rendering pods not usable in disconnected environments.  Instead, create
infra containers with an overlay root FS on `/usr/libexec/pause`.

Signed-off-by: Valentin Rothberg <rothberg@redhat.com>

_Please refer to the individual commits for further details._

comments.body: @containers/podman-maintainers PTAL

> Why do we have to build this image? I would expect that we can just use the hosts rootfs with this binary instead of adding a new image?

We could do that as well.  In that case, the pause binary needs a dedicated directory.  Currently it's in `.../libexec/pause`, so we could turn it into `.../libexec/pause/pause` and run the infra container with `--rootfs .../libexec/pause`.
Sounds good to me.

```
podman (pause) $ ./bin/podman run --rootfs /usr/libexec/podman/ /pause                      
Error: error creating mtab directory: mkdir /usr/libexec/podman/etc: permission denied
```

Looks like --rootfs is not the way to go.

I tried something like this for my rootless cni work, if I remember correctly you have to create an empty writeable directory (ideally on tmpfs) and then bind mount the pause binary into this. This should allow you to use this dir as rootfs.

Try:

./bin/podman run --rootfs /usr/libexec/podman:O /pause

You can even play around with 

# podman run -ti --rootfs /:O sh
@vrothberg did my suggestion on an overlay mount on /usr/libexec/pause/pause work?

> @vrothberg did my suggestion on an overlay mount on /usr/libexec/pause/pause work?

Yes, thanks.  The overlay feature doesn't seem to be documented for `--rootfs` though.  I played with it prior to this PR but I refrained from doing it after the error.

> > @vrothberg did my suggestion on an overlay mount on /usr/libexec/pause/pause work?
> 
> Yes, thanks. The overlay feature doesn't seem to be documented for `--rootfs` though. I played with it prior to this PR but I refrained from doing it after the error.

@vrothberg Oh it seems I only added it with run manual https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html . Please let me know if its needs to be documented at any other place as well. I'll do it :smiley:

> @vrothberg Oh it seems I only added it with run manual https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html . Please let me know if its needs to be documented at any other place as well. I'll do it

Thanks, @flouthoc!  I have the 3.4 man pages installed, so I didn't see it :^)
Back to WIP until the marshaling question is addressed.

> > I really like the changes you made to how/when specgen is filled out, I had been trying to think of a succinct way to move everything around! Only point of discussion is, we could use this as an opportunity (breaking change incoming) to marshal into the infra spec when using the API, could remove a lot of repetitive code and streamline the logic.
> 
> I would absolutely love that. This PR attempts to tackle parts of the code scattering but if we'd allow for the infra spec being marshaled, we could get rid of quite some additional/redundant code.

My concern is, that this creates duplicated fields in the pod and infra spec, e.g. I could set network options on both the pod and infra spec. This conflict is a very bad user experience, it was also not clear which one would be used.
This effects all pod options which should only be set with an infra container. So if we want to use the infra spec via the API we should remove all fields from the pod spec which are already in the infra spec.
That sounds like a fair concern.  Let's leave it like it is for now.  If we desired to change, it should be a separate PR.
@vrothberg are you removing the WIP flag?

> @vrothberg are you removing the WIP flag?

Done.  Let's see if CI is as happy as I am with the changes; the code is fragile.
Gotta wish harder for a happy CI next time @vrothberg .  All kinds of red unhappiness in the CI.

> Gotta wish harder for a happy CI next time @vrothberg . All kinds of red unhappiness in the CI.

But why? :sob:

In-container tests are barking because /tmp is not a tmpfs.  Other tests using overlay mounts do:
```
if os.Getenv("container") != "" {
        Skip("Overlay mounts not supported when running in a container")
}
```

But I think they are supported, just CI won't pass with it.
@rhatdan, regarding portability.  Are we positive that runroot is _always_ on a tmpfs?  If not, we shouldn't use an overlay rootfs and probably go back to building a local image.

If runroot is not a tmpfs Podman's restart handling is fundamentally broken. You may see this in CI, I do not think you will ever see it in the wild.

Only possible exception I can see is some systems which use systemd-tmpfiles to clean out `/tmp` (when it's not a tmpfs).

Disabling `tmpfs` on `/tmp` is something done at image-build time, out of concern of limited memory resources and less-than-perfect root/runroot cleanup by tests (also image cache).  From a "We should be testing how users use" perspective, we really probably shouldn't be doing this.  Tests should be changed to use `/var/tmp` (IIRC, guaranteed never a `tmpfs`) for the root/runroot  and when building VM images, we should leave `/tmp` as-is by the distro.  Then it would be safe to run the containerized tests with a `--tmpfs /tmp`, and the setup would remain similar to non-containerized tests for debug-ability.

Oh, podman-in-container is another awkward one. I still lean towards `runroot` not being on a TMPFS being very dangerous because it leads to fundamentally broken handling of system or container restart.

> You may see this in CI

In CI, the integration tests use a dedicated root/runroot/crio `/tmp` directory tree per test.
It worked!  Now there's just the rootless farts left.  Thanks everybody for the quick resolution!

@vrothberg for the final errors, I recall some very specific dancing is necessary in terms of when `--userns` is converted from a string to an actual NS and UID/GID mappings (or default if on auto) At one point this happened all the way up in cmd/podman so if you moved that too far down the stack that could be causing your issue

> @vrothberg for the final errors, I recall some very specific dancing is necessary in terms of when `--userns` is converted from a string to an actual NS and UID/GID mappings (or default if on auto) At one point this happened all the way up in cmd/podman so if you moved that too far down the stack that could be causing your issue

@cdoern, could you give it a shot?  I am absolutely clueless what's missing.

I think this is needed to set the userns
```diff
diff --git a/pkg/specgen/generate/pod_create.go b/pkg/specgen/generate/pod_create.go
index e0c4a4bf4..e32d53250 100644
--- a/pkg/specgen/generate/pod_create.go
+++ b/pkg/specgen/generate/pod_create.go
@@ -71,7 +71,7 @@ func makeInfraSpec(p *entities.PodSpec, rt *libpod.Runtime) error {
                ep := "/pause"
                infraOptions.Entrypoint = &ep
        }
-
+       p.PodSpecGen.InfraContainerSpec.UserNS = p.PodSpecGen.Userns
        if err := specgenutil.FillOutSpecGen(p.PodSpecGen.InfraContainerSpec, infraOptions, []string{}); err != nil {
                return err
        }
```

However I now run into another error when I try to start the pod
```
$ bin/podman pod create  --userns auto  --name p22
561852ec18b3597a113019e68c6083eeeb692c930951382696ce33aa31eca2bb
$ bin/podman run --pod p22 alpine cat /proc/self/uid_map 
ERRO[0000] Starting some container dependencies         
ERRO[0000] "mount `devpts` to `/dev/pts`: Invalid argument: OCI runtime error" 
Error: error starting some containers: internal libpod error
```

OK It looks like rootfs with overlayfs does not work with userns auto: 
```
podman run  --userns auto --rootfs /usr/:O cat /proc/self/uid_map
Error: OCI runtime error: mount `devpts` to `/dev/pts`: Invalid argument
```
Yes it probably will not work, because we can not shift the file system

Shall we go back to building a local image? A future improvement would be a
scratch container plus bind-mounted pause.

On Thu 21 Oct 2021 at 21:13, Daniel J Walsh ***@***.***>
wrote:

> Yes it probably will not work, because we can not shift the file system
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub

>
>

> Yes it probably will not work, because we can not shift the file system

if we make sure the pause binary is world accessible/readable, we won't need to shift it

> Error: OCI runtime error: mount `devpts` to `/dev/pts`: Invalid argument

this smells like `gid=5` is not valid because there are not enough IDs available.

Are the options still passed down correctly?

e.g. `--userns auto:size=5000` must create a user namespace with 5000 IDs inside.
+1 on going back to building the image locally then we can address the issues with userns=auto separately as it doesn't look related to this feature

> I think this is needed to set the userns

I tried that already but I keep getting the same errors.
Back to building an image.  I also refrained from consolidating the infra-spec gen code any further since it's super fragile.

Hallelujah :pray: 

It's green.  Once @giuseppe's https://github.com/openSUSE/catatonit/pull/17 is merged (and released) we can update the code, remove `pause` entirely and use `catatonit` instead.

The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)

/lgtm
I would like to make the --rootfs pause/:/:O so that the pause image is not viewable to the user.  But for now, this is a big improvement.

> /lgtm I would like to make the --rootfs pause/:/:O so that the pause image is not viewable to the user. But for now, this is a big improvement.

Yes, that would be ideal.  Also, I loved @giuseppe's idea to implement the notion of a `scratch` image. Similar to `buildah from scratch` we could do a `podman run -v pause:/pause scratch`.
Yes that is a neat idea. Might be useful for lots of static use cases.
